passage: A researcher studying the effects of caffeine on cognitive performance concluded that moderate caffeine consumption improves sustained attention but does not enhance creative problem-solving. The researcher based this conclusion on performance tests administered to participants who consumed either 200 mg of caffeine or a placebo before completing tasks designed to measure different cognitive domains.
question: Which finding, if true, would most directly support the researcher's conclusion?

choices.A: Participants who consumed caffeine completed the sustained attention task with significantly fewer errors than placebo participants, but both groups performed equally on the divergent thinking task.
choices.B: Participants who consumed caffeine outperformed placebo participants on all cognitive tasks regardless of the domain being measured.
choices.C: Neither group showed significant performance differences on any cognitive task, suggesting that 200 mg is below the threshold for cognitive effects.
choices.D: Caffeine consumers reported feeling more alert than placebo consumers but did not score higher on any objective performance measure.

explanation: The researcher's conclusion has two parts: caffeine improves sustained attention but does not improve creative problem-solving. Choice A directly supports both parts—caffeine helped on the attention task but not on the divergent thinking (creative) task. B contradicts the 'no enhancement of creative problem-solving' part. C contradicts the 'improves sustained attention' part. D supports neither improvement claim because no objective performance difference is shown.
